content:
# Numbers Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 158-159 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0119
**Source Images:** `NUM_009_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_009_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they had come out of the land of Egypt, and said:

**2** "The Sons of Israel are to celebrate the Passover at its appointed time.

**3** On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, you are to celebrate it at its appointed time. You must observe it according to all its statutes and all its regulations."

**4** So Moses told the Sons of Israel to celebrate the Passover.

**5** And they celebrated the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month, at twilight, in the wilderness of Sinai. The Sons of Israel did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

**6** But there were certain men who had become ritually unclean through contact with a dead body and were unable to celebrate the Passover on that day. They came before Moses and Aaron that same day

**7** and said to him, "We are unclean because of contact with a dead body. Why should we be excluded and not be allowed to present YHWH's offering at its appointed time along with the rest of the Sons of Israel?"

**8** Moses answered them, "Wait here while I find out what YHWH will command concerning you."

**9** YHWH spoke to Moses and said:

**10** "Tell the Sons of Israel: When any one of you or your descendants becomes unclean through contact with a dead body, or is away on a distant journey, that person may still celebrate the Passover to YHWH.

**11** They are to celebrate it in the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight. They must eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.

**12** They are not to leave any of it until morning, nor break any of its bones. They must observe it according to every statute of the Passover.

**13** But anyone who is ritually clean and is not on a journey, yet fails to celebrate the Passover -- that person must be cut off from his people, because he did not present YHWH's offering at its appointed time. That person will bear the consequences of his sin.

**14** If a foreigner residing among you wishes to celebrate the Passover to YHWH, he must observe it according to the statute and regulation of the Passover. There is to be one law for all of you -- for the foreigner and for the native-born alike."

**15** On the day the Tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered the Tabernacle -- the Tent of the Testimony. From evening until morning, the appearance over the Tabernacle was like fire.

**16** So it continued: the cloud covered it by day, and an appearance of fire by night.

**17** Whenever the cloud lifted from above the Tent, the Sons of Israel would set out on their journey. And wherever the cloud settled, there the Sons of Israel would make camp.

**18** At YHWH's command the Sons of Israel would set out, and at YHWH's command they would make camp. As long as the cloud rested over the Tabernacle, they remained in camp.

**19** Even when the cloud lingered over the Tabernacle for many days, the Sons of Israel kept YHWH's charge and did not set out.

**20** Sometimes the cloud remained over the Tabernacle for only a few days. At YHWH's command they would camp, and at YHWH's command they would set out.

**21** Sometimes the cloud stayed only from evening until morning. When the cloud lifted in the morning, they set out. Whether by day or by night, whenever the cloud lifted, they set out.

**22** Whether for two days, or a month, or an extended period -- as long as the cloud lingered over the Tabernacle, resting upon it, the Sons of Israel remained in camp and did not set out. But when it lifted, they set out.

**23** At YHWH's command they camped, and at YHWH's command they set out. They kept YHWH's charge, according to YHWH's command through Moses.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1-5 -- The Passover command and its chronological dislocation** (Heb. *va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh be-midbar Sinai ba-shanah ha-shenit le-tzetam me-eretz Mitzrayim ba-chodesh ha-rishon*): This opening verse presents one of the most discussed chronological anomalies in the Torah. The date given -- the first month of the second year -- is earlier than the census of Numbers 1:1, which is dated to the second month of the second year. The book of Numbers therefore does not begin in chronological order. The rabbis derived from this the principle *ein mukdam u-me'uchar ba-Torah* ("there is no strict chronological sequence in the Torah," Pesachim 6b), a hermeneutical key that unlocks much of the Pentateuch's narrative logic. The placement here, following the Levitical consecration of chapter 8, is thematic rather than temporal: having prepared the cultic personnel, the text now revisits the foundational cultic act -- the Passover that inaugurated Israel's existence as a people. The phrase *ba-mo'ado* ("at its appointed time") occurs three times in verses 2-3, hammering home the importance of temporal precision in ritual observance. The Leningrad Codex on page 158 (continuing from chapter 8) presents the parashah marker clearly before verse 1. The Vaticanus (pa_0119, second column) reads *kai epoiēsan hoi huioi Israēl to pascha* ("and the Sons of Israel made the Passover"), following the Hebrew closely. The phrase *bein ha-arbayim* ("between the evenings," rendered here as "at twilight") is one of the most debated temporal expressions in biblical Hebrew -- the Pharisaic tradition understood it as the period between the sun's decline and darkness (roughly 3-6 PM), while the Samaritan and Sadducean traditions understood it as the brief interval between sunset and full dark. The dynamic rendering "at twilight" captures the liminal quality of the time without resolving the ancient dispute.

**v. 6-8 -- The case of the unclean men** (Heb. *va-yehi anashim asher hayu teme'im le-nefesh adam*): This is one of the rare instances in the Torah where legal innovation arises from a specific human complaint rather than divine initiative. The unnamed men (*anashim*) who are impure by reason of contact with a corpse (*teme'im le-nefesh adam*, literally "unclean for a human soul") present a genuine dilemma: their impurity is not the result of sin but of a necessary human obligation -- caring for the dead. The verb *va-yiqrevu* ("they drew near") in verse 6 is the same root as *qorban* ("offering"), subtly connecting their approach to Moses with the very sacrificial system from which they feel excluded. Their plea -- *lammah nigara* ("why should we be diminished/excluded?") -- uses the verb *gara'* ("to diminish, subtract"), expressing the felt loss of being cut off from communal worship. They do not demand their rights; they lament their deprivation. Moses' response is remarkable in its humility: *imdu ve-eshme'ah mah yetzavveh YHWH lakhem* ("stand and I will hear what YHWH will command for you"). Moses does not improvise a solution or apply existing law by analogy. He waits for fresh revelation. This models a posture of interpretive restraint -- the leader who admits he does not yet know the answer is more faithful than one who forces premature closure. The Vaticanus reads *hestēkate kai akousomai ti enteletai kurios peri humōn* -- "stand and I will hear what the Lord commands concerning you" -- a faithful rendering.

**v. 10-12 -- The second Passover** (Heb. *ba-chodesh ha-sheni be-arba'ah asar yom bein ha-arbayim ya'asu oto*): The institution of a second Passover (*Pesach Sheni*) exactly one month later is one of the Torah's most pastorally sensitive provisions. It communicates that exclusion from sacred time is not permanent -- there is always a second chance to participate in the community's defining ritual. The conditions that trigger the provision are two: corpse-impurity (*tamei la-nefesh*) and distance (*be-derekh rechokah*, "on a distant journey"). Both represent legitimate impediments, not willful neglect. The requirement that the second Passover be observed with all the same elements -- unleavened bread (*matzot*), bitter herbs (*merorim*), no breaking of bones (*ve-etzem lo yishberu vo*), nothing left until morning -- preserves the ritual's integrity. The second Passover is not a lesser observance; it is the full Passover displaced in time. Jewish tradition developed extensive discussion around what constitutes a "distant journey" -- the Mishnah (Pesachim 9:2) defines it as being beyond the threshold of the Temple courtyard at the time of slaughter. The Leningrad Codex text here on page 158 is clearly legible.

**v. 13 -- The severity of willful neglect** (Heb. *ve-ha-ish asher hu tahor u-ve-derekh lo hayah ve-chadal la'asot ha-pesach ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ammeha*): The verb *chadal* ("to cease, refrain, neglect") implies deliberate omission. This person is clean and present -- both impediments have been removed -- yet he simply does not participate. The penalty is *karet* ("cutting off"), the most severe divine punishment in the Torah short of death. The person is not executed by a court but is "cut off from his people" (*ve-nikhretah... me-ammeha*), a mysterious sanction whose precise nature has been debated since antiquity. The Talmud (Keritot 1:1) lists thirty-six offenses punishable by *karet*. Willful failure to observe Passover ranks alongside the most serious transgressions -- eating on Yom Kippur, consuming blood, performing prohibited labor on the Sabbath. The reason is stated explicitly: *ki qorban YHWH lo hiqriv be-mo'ado* ("because he did not present YHWH's offering at its appointed time"). Passover is not optional. It is the one festival whose neglect carries the penalty of communal severance, because Passover constitutes Israel's identity: to refuse the Passover is, in effect, to refuse membership in the people YHWH redeemed. The added phrase *chet'o yissa* ("his sin he will bear") intensifies the warning -- the consequences are not externally imposed but internally carried. The LXX renders this *hamartian autou lēmpsetai* ("he shall receive his sin"), preserving the sense of personal burden.

**v. 14 -- One law for the foreigner** (Heb. *chuqqah achat yihyeh lakhem ve-la-ger u-le-ezrach ha-aretz*): The inclusion of the *ger* ("resident foreigner") in Passover observance is one of the Torah's most expansive provisions. The *ger* is not a casual visitor but someone who has attached themselves to Israel's community. The phrase *chuqqah achat* ("one statute") establishes legal equality in sacred observance between the foreigner and the *ezrach* ("native-born"). This is remarkable given the exclusionary possibilities -- Passover celebrates Israel's unique deliverance from Egypt, an event the foreigner's ancestors did not experience. Yet the Torah insists: participation in the community's defining memory is open to those who join the community. The foreigner enters Israel's story by entering Israel's ritual. The Vaticanus follows this precisely. This verse anticipates the prophetic vision of Isaiah 56:3-7, where the foreigner who joins himself to YHWH will not be separated from the covenant people.

**v. 15-16 -- The cloud and fire over the Tabernacle** (Heb. *u-ve-yom haqim et ha-Mishkan kissah he-anan et ha-Mishkan le-ohel ha-edut*): The chapter's second half pivots sharply from Passover legislation to the theology of divine guidance. The cloud (*anan*) that covers the Tabernacle on the day it is erected connects this passage to Exodus 40:34-38, forming a narrative resumption after the intervening legislation. The cloud is identified with *ohel ha-edut* ("the Tent of the Testimony"), not the more common *ohel mo'ed* ("Tent of Meeting") -- a shift in terminology that emphasizes the Tabernacle as the repository of the covenant tablets (the *edut*, "testimony"). The nighttime appearance is described as *ke-mar'eh esh* ("like the appearance of fire"), not fire itself but something that looked like fire. The qualification is important: the narrator carefully avoids saying the Tabernacle was literally ablaze. What hovered above it had the visual quality of fire -- luminous, dynamic, alive -- without being combustion. This echoes the burning bush of Exodus 3:2 (*ha-seneh bo'er ba-esh ve-ha-seneh einennu ukkal* -- "the bush was burning with fire but the bush was not consumed"), establishing a consistent visual grammar for theophany. The Leningrad Codex presents this section beginning in the right column of page 158, with the Vaticanus text on pa_0119 (third column) reading *kai enephelen hē nephelē kai ekaluphsen autēn* -- "and the cloud shadowed and covered it." The LXX adds *enephelen* ("cast shadow"), an interpretive expansion not present in the Hebrew, emphasizing the cloud's protective, sheltering function.

**v. 17-18 -- Movement governed by the cloud** (Heb. *u-le-fi he'alot he-anan me-al ha-ohel ve-acharei khen yis'u benei Yisrael*): The verb *he'alot* ("lifting up") applied to the cloud is the same root as the lampstand-lighting verb of 8:2 (*be-ha'alotekha*), creating a subtle verbal link between the two chapters: the flame that ascends in the lampstand and the cloud that ascends from the Tabernacle share the same upward motion. The phrase *al pi YHWH* ("at the mouth of YHWH," rendered "at YHWH's command") occurs eight times in verses 18-23, a concentration unmatched anywhere else in the Torah. The repetition is not redundancy but liturgical insistence: every departure and every encampment is governed by divine speech. Israel moves when YHWH speaks; Israel rests when YHWH speaks. There is no autonomy of movement. The wilderness journey is, in its totality, an act of obedience to the voice. The Leningrad Codex on page 159 presents these verses in the left column with characteristic clarity. The Vaticanus reads *dia phōnēs kuriou* ("through the voice of the Lord") for *al pi YHWH*, interpreting *pi* ("mouth") as *phōnē* ("voice") -- a slight shift from the oral/physical image of a divine mouth to the auditory image of a divine voice.

**v. 19-22 -- The discipline of waiting** (Heb. *u-ve-ha'arikh he-anan al ha-Mishkan yamim rabbim*): These verses describe four distinct scenarios of cloud duration: many days (v. 19), a few days (v. 20), evening to morning (v. 21), and an extended period -- *yamayim o chodesh o yamim* ("two days or a month or days/a year," v. 22). The Hebrew *yamim* in verse 22 is ambiguous -- it can mean simply "days" (plural) or, in certain contexts, "a year" (as in Lev 25:29, Judg 17:10). The ambiguity is likely intentional: the text refuses to specify maximum duration because the point is that no duration is too long or too short for obedience. The verb *ya'arikh* ("to lengthen, linger") in verse 19 and its cognate *be-he'arikh* in verse 22 share the root *aleph-resh-kaf*, which carries connotations of patience, endurance, and the stretching of time. YHWH's people must learn to live in the tension between readiness and waiting. The threefold repetition of the pattern -- cloud rests, Israel camps; cloud lifts, Israel journeys -- creates a rhythmic, almost hypnotic cadence that mirrors the experience itself: the monotony and discipline of following a cloud through trackless wilderness. The phrase *ve-shameru benei Yisrael et mishmeret YHWH ve-lo yissa'u* ("the Sons of Israel kept YHWH's charge and did not set out," v. 19) uses the *shamar/mishmeret* ("keep/charge") vocabulary of sentinel duty. Waiting is not passive inaction but active guardianship -- a watchful stillness that is itself a form of service. The Vaticanus on pa_0119 preserves all four duration scenarios, reading *ē hēmeras ē mēna ē eniaution* for the extended period -- explicitly rendering *yamim* as *eniaution* ("a year"), making the interpretive choice that the Hebrew leaves open.

**v. 23 -- Concluding summary** (Heb. *al pi YHWH yachanu ve-al pi YHWH yissa'u et mishmeret YHWH shamaru al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh*): The final verse contains three occurrences of *al pi YHWH* ("at YHWH's command") and one *be-yad Mosheh* ("through Moses"), packing divine authority and prophetic mediation into a single sentence. The phrase *be-yad* ("by the hand of") is the standard idiom for prophetic transmission -- revelation passes through Moses' hand as through a conduit, not originating with him but channeled through him. The verse functions as a colophon for the entire cloud-guidance unit (vv. 15-23), sealing it with a summary of total obedience. The repetitive structure of this closing section (vv. 15-23) has sometimes been attributed to multiple sources by critical scholars, but the repetition serves a clear rhetorical function: it enacts in prose what it describes in content. The experience of following the cloud was itself repetitive -- wait, move, wait, move -- and the text mirrors that rhythm. The reader who grows weary of the repeated formulae is, in a small way, experiencing what Israel experienced in the wilderness: the discipline of doing the same thing, faithfully, again and again, at YHWH's command.

**Structural note on chapter 9 in its context**: Numbers 9 divides into two sharply distinct sections: the Passover legislation (vv. 1-14) and the cloud-guidance narrative (vv. 15-23). These are often treated as unrelated units joined by editorial seam, but they share a deep thematic unity. Both concern how Israel relates to sacred time. The Passover section asks: what happens when you cannot observe the appointed time? The cloud section asks: who determines when it is time to move and when to stay? In both cases, the answer is the same -- YHWH governs time. The appointed time of Passover is YHWH's (*be-mo'ado*); the appointed time of departure is YHWH's (*al pi YHWH*). Israel does not own its own calendar; it lives within a rhythm established and sustained by divine command. The chapter thus bridges the cultic legislation of chapters 1-8 and the departure narrative that begins in 10:11, when the cloud finally lifts and Israel sets out from Sinai -- at last, at YHWH's command, at the appointed time.
